skill.name: chinese-learning-assistant
skill.description: Helps intermediate Chinese learners analyze natural usage, improve written Chinese, and verify examples from native web sources.
skill.summary: Analyze Chinese expressions and rewrite learner text into more natural, fluent Chinese.
skill.author: 0918nobita

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many Ruby backtick, weak cryptography, and high-entropy findings. Manual review found these are false positives from Markdown code fences, inline search-query examples, and Japanese or Chinese instructional text, not executable code or obfuscation. The only meaningful operational risk is low: the skill asks the assistant to use web search for real language examples, so users should avoid submitting private text.

low_findings.title: Web Search May Expose User-Provided Text
low_findings.description: The skill instructs the assistant to search external sites for native usage examples. This is useful for language learning, but user-submitted phrases or draft sentences may be sent to search providers or public websites.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The instructions clearly require web search for examples, so network exposure is real. I found no evidence that secrets are requested or exfiltrated, which keeps severity low.
low_findings.recommendation: Warn users not to include private, confidential, or identifying text when asking for web-verified examples.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Examples Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged Ruby or shell backtick execution across SKILL.md and references/search-guide.md. Manual review shows these lines are Markdown code fences and inline search-query examples, not executable scripts.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines contain documentation examples such as quoted search terms and output formats. I found no executable Ruby, shell script, subprocess call, or command interpolation context.
low_findings.recommendation: No security fix is required for these Markdown examples.

low_findings.title: Description Text Misclassified as Weak Cryptography
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged SKILL.md line 3 as weak cryptography. That line is the skill description in Japanese and contains no cryptographic API, algorithm name, or hashing operation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact line is frontmatter description text for a Chinese learning assistant. There is no semantic evidence of MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or any cryptographic use.

low_findings.title: Multilingual Markdown Misclassified as Obfuscation
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged high entropy in SKILL.md and three reference files. Manual review shows readable Japanese and Chinese learning materials, not packed, encrypted, or binary payloads.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files are plain Markdown with Japanese headings and Chinese examples, which can raise entropy heuristics. I found no encoded blob, compressed payload, or hidden executable content.
low_findings.recommendation: No remediation is needed; entropy scoring should be interpreted carefully for multilingual text.

content.value_statement: Intermediate learners often know grammar but still write stiff or unnatural Chinese. This skill explains real usage and rewrites text into natural Chinese with source-backed examples.
actual_capabilities: Analyzes Chinese expressions by formality, spoken or written style, audience, and common usage context.
Explains why an expression sounds natural or unnatural using learner-friendly language.
Suggests natural alternatives, variants, and formality shifts for Chinese phrases.
Rewrites learner compositions for fluency, tone, rhythm, and practical communication.
Uses web search guidance to find recent examples from platforms such as Weibo, bilibili, Zhihu, and Douban.
Provides structured feedback for chat messages, translation practice, essays, and longer writing.
limitations: It depends on web search availability when current examples are required.
It is aimed at HSK4-level learners and may not cover beginner grammar in depth.
It focuses on Mandarin usage and may not fully handle dialect-specific language.
It cannot guarantee that public web examples are correct without careful source review.

use_cases.title: Make Chat Messages Sound Natural
use_cases.description: Rewrite a draft message for a friend, classmate, or native speaker with the right tone and level of casualness.

use_cases.title: Understand Expressions From Videos
use_cases.description: Analyze a phrase from comments, subtitles, or posts and learn where it is appropriate to use it.

use_cases.title: Improve Translation Practice
use_cases.description: Move from direct translation to natural Chinese phrasing with explanations of word choice and sentence flow.

prompt_templates.title: Explain a New Expression
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze the Chinese expression "[expression]". Explain when people use it, who can use it, and whether it is casual or formal.

prompt_templates.title: Rewrite a Short Message
prompt_templates.prompt: Please improve this Chinese message for a friend. Keep it casual and natural: [your Chinese text].

prompt_templates.title: Compare Similar Phrases
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare these Chinese expressions: [phrase A], [phrase B], and [phrase C]. Explain the nuance, formality, and best situations for each.

prompt_templates.title: Polish a Full Composition
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Chinese composition for fluency. Give natural rewrites, explain the changes, and include recent native usage examples where helpful: [composition].

output_examples.output: The skill explains that 搞定了 is casual spoken Chinese for finishing or resolving something. It gives suitable contexts, more formal alternatives, and native-style examples.

output_examples.input: 我昨天去了电影院，看了一个很好的电影。
output_examples.output: The skill suggests a more natural sentence such as 我昨天去看电影了，那部电影挺好看的. It explains the verb phrase, measure word, and softer spoken tone.

output_examples.input: Compare 我认为 and 我觉得.
output_examples.output: The skill explains that 我觉得 is more common in conversation, while 我认为 sounds more formal or deliberate. It gives examples for chat, essays, and discussion.

best_practices: Tell the skill your target audience, setting, and desired formality before asking for a rewrite.
Ask for several alternatives when you need to choose between casual, neutral, and formal Chinese.
Use web-verified examples for slang, recent phrases, and expressions from social media.
anti_patterns: Do not submit private messages or confidential text when asking for web search examples.
Do not treat every public web example as correct without checking context and source quality.
Do not ask only for grammar correction if your goal is natural native-like fluency.

faq.question: What level is this skill for?
faq.answer: It is designed for learners around HSK4 who want more natural and fluent Chinese.

faq.question: Can it correct Chinese writing?
faq.answer: Yes. It rewrites learner text for natural wording, tone, flow, and practical communication.

faq.question: Does it explain slang from bilibili or social media?
faq.answer: Yes. It can analyze casual expressions and use web examples when current usage matters.

faq.question: Does it support formal Chinese?
faq.answer: Yes. It can compare casual, neutral, and formal versions of an expression.

faq.question: Does it require web access?
faq.answer: Web access is useful when you need recent native examples. General correction can still work without it.

faq.question: Is it a dictionary replacement?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on practical usage, naturalness, context, and rewrite guidance.
